FRACTIONAL CMO · COMPARISON

Fractional CMO vs head of growth.

Different roles, different shapes, different problems they solve. The most common founder mistake in 2026 marketing hiring is treating these as interchangeable. They aren't. Here's the honest decision frame: what each role is for, what each costs, and which one your company actually needs first.

The TL;DR

The honest one-paragraph answer

Hire a fractional CMO when you need senior marketing leadership — strategy, team structure, brand, budget ownership, board reporting. Hire a head of growth when you have strategy and need an execution leader deep in experimentation, conversion optimization, and growth-loop engineering. For most $2M-$20M companies without a senior marketing leader, you need the fractional CMO first; the head of growth becomes the right next hire once strategic direction is set.

What each is for

The role shapes are different

Fractional CMO

  • Owns: The full marketing function — strategy, brand, positioning, team, budget, vendor relationships, board communication.
  • Time commitment: Part-time, typically 1-3 days per week. $8K-$25K/month.
  • Engagement length: 6-18 months typical.
  • Best when: The company needs senior marketing leadership but isn't ready for or can't afford a full-time CMO.
  • The deliverable shape: A working marketing function (strategy + team + systems + budget) operating against revenue goals.

Head of growth

  • Owns: A specific stage of the funnel — usually acquisition (paid + organic + SEO) or activation/retention loops. Sometimes both.
  • Time commitment: Full-time. $140K-$220K base + bonus + equity (early stage).
  • Engagement length: Permanent hire.
  • Best when: Strategy is clear; the company needs deep execution on conversion, experimentation, and channel optimization.
  • The deliverable shape: Compounding growth loops, lift on specific funnel metrics, repeatable experimentation framework.
Side-by-side

Direct comparison

DimensionFractional CMOHead of growth
Role shapePart-time senior leadershipFull-time IC or team lead
ScopeFull marketing functionOne-to-two stages of the funnel
Strategy ownershipYes — primaryWithin their scope only
Brand + positioningYesUsually no
Team managementYes — builds and leads the teamManages growth team if one exists
Budget ownershipYes — full marketing budgetGrowth/paid budget subset
Board reportingYesRarely
Experimentation depthLighter — directionalDeeper — operational
Analytics depthSenior overviewDeep, in the tooling daily
Cost (annual)$100K-$300K all-in$180K-$300K fully-loaded + equity
Time to valueWeek 2-3Month 4-6 (search + ramp)
Engagement length6-18 months typicalPermanent
The common founder mistake

Hiring head of growth when you needed a CMO

The most-common pattern we see at Treetop: a founder hires a head of growth at $200K total comp, expecting them to "own marketing." Six months in, the head of growth is burning out trying to do work they weren't hired for — brand, positioning, vendor management, board reporting — while their actual growth work is suffering because they're spread across the function.

The head of growth either quits (loud signal: "I'm not getting set up for success"), gets quietly relabeled "VP marketing" and underperforms because they were hired for something else, or stays in the role but stops doing the experimentation work they were exceptional at.

The honest signal: If you can't articulate to a candidate what your marketing strategy is, your brand positioning is, your ICP is, and your annual marketing plan looks like — you don't yet need a head of growth. You need a CMO (full-time or fractional) to set those things first.

The sequencing answer

When to hire which (and in what order)

Sequence A: Most common (recommended)

  • Month 0: Hire fractional CMO. They set strategy, build team, install systems, define KPIs.
  • Month 6-12: Hire first full-time marketing manager / specialist roles based on the strategy.
  • Month 12-18: If growth-loop engineering is the next constraint, hire head of growth (full-time) as the fractional CMO transitions out or steps down.
  • Month 18-24: Possibly hire a full-time CMO if scale justifies it (typically $20M+ revenue).

Sequence B: When you already have strategy clarity

  • Month 0: Hire head of growth (full-time) to own experimentation and channel optimization against an already-defined strategy.
  • Month 6-12: Hire fractional CMO if you discover you need senior strategic ownership beyond what the head of growth can take on.

Sequence B is rarer than founders think. Most companies that believe they "already have strategy clarity" actually have an idea, not a defined strategy. The fractional CMO discovery process usually surfaces this within 30 days.

When both is the right answer

Running fractional CMO and head of growth together

For larger mid-market companies ($10M-$30M), running both roles in parallel often works well: the fractional CMO owns strategy + brand + team structure + board communication; the head of growth owns experimentation + channel optimization + growth loops. The fractional CMO unblocks the head of growth's strategic dependencies; the head of growth executes within the strategic frame the fractional CMO sets.

The dynamic only breaks when the roles aren't clearly delineated — at which point both leaders will tell you, separately, that the structure doesn't work.

Not sure which role you need?
